planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall on the surface of moist, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun; seeds require exposure to winter moisture and temperature fluctuations to germinate reliably in spring.

directions: Mohr's Thoroughwort is a native perennial boneset of wet longleaf pine flatwoods and seepage slopes along the Gulf Coast, from Alabama through Florida, where it grows in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun. Plant in consistently moist to seasonally wet conditions and avoid letting the soil dry out, especially during the first growing season while plants establish their root systems. From late summer through fall, flat-topped clusters of tiny fluffy white composite flowers attract a remarkable diversity of native bees, wasps, and butterflies. An excellent companion to other wet savanna natives such as Liatris, Helianthus heterophyllus, and Carphephorus species in flatwoods restoration and rain garden plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall on the surface of moist, acidic soil; the distinctive white phyllary bracts are visible even before flowers open and help confirm identification in the garden.

directions: White-bracted Thoroughwort is a native perennial of wet, acidic meadows, bogs, and coastal plain pinelands with a range extending from Massachusetts south to Florida, making it one of the more cold-hardy wet-soil Eupatoriums available to northern gardeners. The flat-topped clusters of white composite flowers that appear in late summer and fall are distinguished by strikingly bright white phyllary bracts that give the plant an unusually crisp, clean appearance compared to other species in the genus. Grow in moist to wet, sandy or peaty, acidic soil in full sun and pair with other wet meadow natives for a naturalistic rain garden or low-area planting that provides exceptional late-season support for native bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown transplants in spring into dry, rocky clay-loam or well-drained open soil in full sun; source only from nurseries with legally propagated stock, as this is a federally endangered species.

directions: Schweinitz's Sunflower is a federally endangered perennial sunflower endemic to the piedmont of North and South Carolina, where it persists on a small number of dry, rocky clay-loam roadsides and openings in oak-hickory forest that experience periodic disturbance historically provided by fire and bison grazing. Tall, branching stems with large, rough, sessile leaves support bright yellow flowers 2.5 to 3 inches across that open from September through October, providing critical nectar for monarch butterflies during fall migration and seeds relished by goldfinches and other finches. Grow in poor, dry, open, well-drained soil in full sun with excellent drainage — this species is notably intolerant of wet or compacted conditions and actually performs better in lean soil without supplemental fertilizer. Purchase only from specialty native plant nurseries carrying legally propagated stock; cultivating this plant from nursery-grown transplants is an important act of conservation for one of North America's rarest sunflowers.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown transplants in spring into dry, rocky, well-drained soil; cold stratification of 60–90 days improves seed germination if starting from seed, but transplants are the practical route to a flowering plant.

directions: Kidneyleaf Rosinweed is one of the most architecturally striking native perennials of the piedmont and Blue Ridge of the Carolinas and Virginia, where it grows on rocky, dry outcrops and open, well-drained woodland edges and granite barrens. The plant produces a single enormous, deeply kidney-shaped to cordate basal leaf up to a foot across, from which rises a tall, slender, almost leafless flowering stem topped with bright yellow, daisy-like flowers about 2 inches across that open in July and August. Once the deep taproot is established — a process that takes two to three years from a young transplant — the plant becomes remarkably drought-tolerant and virtually self-sufficient in lean, rocky soil without supplemental irrigation or fertilizer. The resinous seeds are relished by goldfinches, and the unusual growth form — a vast basal leaf paired with a wiry vertical stem — makes this an outstanding structural focal point in rock gardens, dry native meadows, and open woodland settings.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall on the surface of dry, sandy, acidic soil in full sun; seeds are light-dependent germinators and should not be covered — simply press onto the soil surface and allow winter rains to settle them in place.

directions: Cottony Goldenaster is a softly woolly native perennial of dry, open, sandy pinelands, scrub, and coastal plain barrens from North Carolina south through Florida and west to Alabama, where its dense covering of cottony white hairs gives the whole plant a distinctive silvery-white appearance even before it blooms. From September through December — among the latest-blooming native asters in the Southeast — the plant produces masses of small, bright golden-yellow daisy-like flowers that serve as a critical late-season nectar source for lingering monarch butterflies and native bees building their winter reserves. Plant in dry, sandy, acidic, extremely well-drained soil in full sun and avoid clay soils or any site prone to standing water, which will quickly rot the crown. Once established, Cottony Goldenaster is highly drought-tolerant and requires little care beyond removing spent stems in late winter to make way for new basal growth.
